text: During the exploration of the abdominal cavity, tumor implants are visible in the right flank, right iliac fossa, right colon serosa, and right fallopian tube. The surgeon performs a right parietal peritonectomy, dissecting the peritoneum and retroperitoneum up to the parietocolic junction. Mucinous implants on the right colon serosa are excised. The right ovarian vessels are identified and sectioned, followed by a right salpingo-oophorectomy with oncological margins. The right parietal peritonectomy is completed while preserving the right round uterine ligament.

The surgeon performs a left salpingo-oophorectomy, followed by reinforcement of the excised surface on the right colon wall using interrupted sutures. The appendicular stump is resected with an endogia stapler. The patient's position is adjusted to an anti-Trendelenburg orientation to facilitate access to the upper abdomen, where the hepatic round ligament is removed.

The surgeon begins the complete omentectomy by opening the transcavity from the major gastric curvature, proceeding towards the right side. The gastroepiploic vessels are carefully dissected and preserved. The right omentectomy is completed by following the transverse colon up to the hepatic colonic flexure. The left omentectomy starts from the major curvature, extending to the splenic hilum, and continues along the transverse colon up to the splenic flexure, completing the total omentectomy.

Following complete cytoreduction, the surgeon employs a HIPEC CO₂ close technique device for administering hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy. Cannulas are inserted through laparoscopic ports, and mitomycin is administered for pseudomyxoma treatment. A transparent device is used in the umbilical port to monitor chemotherapy levels during the CO₂ agitation process.
